title: Unit 01 Pretest
description: Diagnostic pretest for Economic Thinking and Scarcity.

choices.text: The study of how people use limited resources to meet needs and wants
choices.feedback: Correct. Economics examines how individuals and societies make choices when resources are limited.

choices.text: The study of how governments print and control money
choices.feedback: Economics includes much more than government money policies. It studies choices about limited resources.

choices.text: The study of how businesses earn profits only
choices.feedback: Economics includes business decisions, but it also includes individuals, governments, scarcity, and choice.

choices.text: The study of why all people want the same goods and services
choices.feedback: Economics does not assume all people want the same things. It focuses on choices under scarcity.

questions.prompt: Scarcity means resources are limited, so people must make choices.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Scarcity does mean resources are limited. That is why choices must be made.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. Scarcity exists because resources are limited, and that forces decision-making.

questions.prompt: A student chooses to spend Saturday studying instead of working a part-time shift. What is the opportunity cost if the next best alternative was working?

choices.text: The textbook the student used while studying
choices.feedback: A textbook used during studying is not the next best alternative that was given up.

choices.text: All activities the student could ever do on Saturday
choices.feedback: Opportunity cost is not every possible option. It is the next best alternative not chosen.

choices.text: The money and work experience the student gave up by not working
choices.feedback: Correct. Opportunity cost is the value of the next best alternative that was not chosen.

choices.text: The grade the student hopes to earn
choices.feedback: The hoped-for grade is a possible benefit of studying, not the opportunity cost of the decision.

questions.prompt: In a market economy, the government makes all major decisions about production and prices.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Correct. In a market economy, individuals and businesses make most economic decisions.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: That describes a command economy more closely, not a market economy.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is the best example of a positive incentive?

choices.text: A school removes extra recess because students were late
choices.feedback: This is a penalty or consequence, so it is a negative incentive, not a positive one.

choices.text: A store runs out of a product because demand increased
choices.feedback: Running out of stock is not an incentive. It is a result of supply and demand conditions.

choices.text: A family must choose between groceries and entertainment
choices.feedback: This example shows scarcity and trade-offs, not a positive incentive.

choices.text: A company offers workers a bonus for perfect attendance
choices.feedback: Correct. A bonus is a reward that encourages a specific behavior, so it is a positive incentive.

questions.prompt: A mixed economy combines elements of market and command systems.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: A mixed economy does combine features of market and command systems.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Correct. A mixed economy includes both private decision-making and government involvement.

questions.prompt: Which example best shows a trade-off?

choices.text: A student receives a scholarship for strong grades
choices.feedback: This example shows a reward or positive incentive, not a trade-off.

choices.text: A business increases prices after production costs rise
choices.feedback: This shows a business response to costs, but it does not directly illustrate choosing between alternatives.

choices.text: A factory uses machines to produce more goods
choices.feedback: This example shows the use of capital resources, not a trade-off between alternatives.

choices.text: A student chooses to buy a calculator instead of saving the money for concert tickets
choices.feedback: Correct. A trade-off involves giving up one option when choosing another.

questions.prompt: Opportunity cost includes every option a person gives up when making a choice.

choices.text: False
choices.feedback: Correct. Opportunity cost refers only to the next best alternative that was not chosen.

choices.text: True
choices.feedback: Opportunity cost is not every option given up. It is the value of the next best alternative.
